question: Precision EW observables on the $Z$ pole at LEP: which inputs over-constrain the SM?

options: Three inputs fix tree-level EW: $\alpha(m_Z), G_F, M_Z$; all other observables predicted including $M_W$, $\sin^2\theta_{\rm eff}^\ell$, $\Gamma_Z$, asymmetries $A_{FB}$, $R_b$ etc. â€” at loop level depend on $m_t, m_h$; comparison gave indirect $m_t \approx 170$ GeV before direct discovery (1995) and $m_h \sim 100$ GeV before LHC found 125 GeV
Only $\alpha$ and $G_F$ needed; $M_Z$ predicted (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
All EW observables independent; nothing constrains anything (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

Inputs are quark masses only; gauge couplings derived (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: Standard SM inputs at EW scale: (1) fine-structure constant at $M_Z$: $\alpha^{-1}(M_Z) \approx 128.94$ (running from $\alpha^{-1}(0) = 137.036$ via hadronic + leptonic loops); (2) Fermi constant $G_F = 1.1664 \times 10^{-5}$ GeV$^{-2}$ from muon lifetime; (3) $M_Z = 91.1876$ GeV from LEP scan. These determine $g, g', v$. Predictions: $M_W = 80.357 \pm 0.006$ GeV (SM); $\sin^2\theta_{\rm eff}^\ell = 0.23152(5)$; $\Gamma_Z = 2.494$ GeV. Pre-LHC: global fit with $M_W, A_{FB}^b, A_{LR}, R_b$ etc.\ pulled $m_t$ to ~170 GeV via $\Delta\rho \propto m_t^2$ â†’ discovered at $173$ GeV (CDF/D0 1995, NUMA Nobel). Pre-Higgs: $m_h$ pulled to $\sim$ 90 GeV (with log dependence) â†’ found at 125 GeV (within ~$2\sigma$ of EW fit).

active.prompt: Match each precision EW observable with its current value:

pairs.definition: 80.369 GeV (PDG 2024); CDF anomaly 2022 ($+7\sigma$ from SM) was not confirmed by ATLAS/LHCb measurements
91.1876(21) GeV; LEP scan precision; defines EW scale
0.23153(4); from $Z$-pole forward-backward asymmetries; tension between $A_{FB}^b$ (LEP) and $A_{LR}$ (SLD)
2.4955(23) GeV total $Z$ width; partial widths to fermion pairs give $N_\nu = 2.984$

active.explanation: Z-pole observables: total width $\Gamma_Z$, partial widths $\Gamma_e, \Gamma_\mu, \Gamma_\tau, \Gamma_q$, asymmetries $A_{FB}^{0,f}, A_e, A_b, A_c$. LEP/SLD measured all to permille precision. Famous tension: $A_{FB}^b$ (LEP) and $A_{LR}$ (SLD) differ by ~$3\sigma$ on $\sin^2\theta_{\rm eff}^\ell$. CDF $M_W$ (2022) = 80.434 GeV: $7\sigma$ above SM. Subsequent ATLAS update (2024) and LHCb (2024) give SM-consistent values â€” tension likely with CDF analysis.

question: Peskin-Takeuchi $S, T, U$ parameters: what BSM physics do they encode?

options: Oblique corrections from new heavy particles modifying gauge boson self-energies (photon, $W$, $Z$). $S$: shift in $\Pi'_{3Y}(0)$, $T$: custodial-symmetry breaking ($\Pi_{WW}(0) - \Pi_{33}(0)$, related to $\Delta\rho$), $U$: weaker, $\Pi'_{33}(0) - \Pi'_{WW}(0)$. Heavy fermion doublets, extra gauge bosons, etc. contribute; current fits $S = -0.02\pm 0.07$, $T = 0.03\pm 0.06$ â€” consistent with SM

S = strangeness, T = top number, U = up flavour; new conservation laws (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship)

Same as Mandelstam variables for new physics (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)

Free parameters of MSSM Lagrangian (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Peskin-Takeuchi (1990): parametrise effect of new heavy physics on EW precision data via three combinations of gauge boson 2-point functions (assuming new physics couples mainly via gauge bosons â€” 'oblique'). $S$: change in vector polarisation, sensitive to new gauge groups, KK gauge bosons. $T$: violation of custodial symmetry, sensitive to non-degenerate fermion doublets (e.g.\ extra quark $(t', b')$ with $\Delta m \neq 0$ gives $T \propto (\Delta m)^2$). $U$: usually small. Current global EW fit (Gfitter, HEPfit): $S = -0.02 \pm 0.07$, $T = 0.03 \pm 0.06$, $U = 0.01 \pm 0.07$. All consistent with SM. Strong constraint on BSM: e.g.\ technicolour gives $S \sim N_{TC}/(6\pi) \sim 0.2$ â€” disfavoured; 4th generation excluded; SUSY OK if not too light.

question: Muon $g-2$ anomaly: what's the discrepancy and current status?

options: Fermilab E989 (2021/23 results combined with BNL E821): $a_\mu^{\rm exp} = 116592059(22)\times 10^{-11}$; SM prediction depends on hadronic vacuum polarisation (HVP): data-driven approach gives $4.2\sigma$ tension, lattice (BMW 2020) reduces tension to $\lesssim 1\sigma$. New BMW + CMD-3 results consistent â€” open theoretical debate, no clear BSM signal yet
Muon g-2 agrees perfectly with SM, no anomaly (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Anomaly at $10\sigma$, clear BSM signal (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence)

Anomaly resolved by relativistic QED corrections in 2022 (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: Muon anomalous moment $a_\mu = (g-2)/2$. SM contributions: QED (5-loop), EW (1-loop), hadronic VP (largest uncertainty), hadronic light-by-light. Experiment: BNL E821 (2001, $a_\mu = (11659208.0\pm 6.3)\times 10^{-10}$) â†’ Fermilab E989 (2021 Run-1, 2023 Run-2+3) confirms and improves. Combined exp average $\sim 200$ ppb. SM theory landscape: (i) Data-driven HVP using $e^+e^- \to$ hadrons gives SM theory initiative 2020 value, $4.2\sigma$ below experiment; (ii) BMW lattice (Nature 2021): HVP higher, reduces tension to $1.6\sigma$ â€” and disagrees with data-driven by $\sim 2.1\sigma$. New CMD-3 (2023) $e^+e^- \to \pi^+\pi^-$ data shifts data-driven up, closer to BMW. Status: open theoretical question; BSM contributions (heavy SUSY, leptoquarks) could explain difference but no other signal yet.

question: Why does $m_t$ contribute quadratically to EW radiative corrections, while $m_h$ contributes only logarithmically?

options: Top Yukawa breaks custodial $SU(2)$ ($y_t \neq y_b$), giving $\Delta\rho \propto m_t^2$ â€” quadratic; Higgs contributes via gauge boson self-energy diagrams with internal Higgs, custodial-preserving for the relevant pieces, giving only log $\ln(m_h^2/m_W^2)$ corrections to $S$, $T$. Veltman's 'screening theorem' formalises this

Both contribute quadratically; just measurement precision differs (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Top doesn't contribute to EW corrections (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

Quadratic dependence is artefact of choice of gauge (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: $\rho$ parameter at 1-loop: $\Delta\rho^{\rm top} = 3G_F m_t^2/(8\pi^2\sqrt 2) \approx 0.01$ â€” quadratic in $m_t$ because top-bottom mass splitting maximally breaks custodial symmetry. Higgs contribution: $\Delta\rho^{\rm Higgs} = -(3G_F M_Z^2 \sin^2\theta_W/8\pi^2\sqrt 2)\ln(m_h^2/M_Z^2) + \ldots$ â€” logarithmic. Veltman screening theorem (1976): in custodial-preserving theory, scalar masses enter only logarithmically into oblique observables. Why? Equivalence theorem: at high energy, Higgs decouples from longitudinal gauge bosons up to log corrections that resum via RGE. Practical implication: $m_t$ pinned tightly by EW fit, $m_h$ only loosely; consistent with EW fits historically pulling $m_t$ to within $\sim$5 GeV of correct value, while $m_h$ uncertain to factor of $\sim$2.

question: EW running: how do gauge couplings $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3$ evolve, and does this hint at unification?

options: $1/\alpha_i(\mu) = 1/\alpha_i(\mu_0) - (b_i/2\pi)\ln(\mu/\mu_0)$ with SM $\beta$-coefficients $(b_1, b_2, b_3) = (41/10, -19/6, -7)$ (with GUT-normalised $\alpha_1 = (5/3)\alpha_Y$); SM running shows the three nearly meet near $10^{14}-10^{16}$ GeV but with significant gap; MSSM coefficients $(33/5, 1, -3)$ give precision unification at $\sim 2\times 10^{16}$ GeV â€” gauge coupling unification

Couplings don't run; constants of nature (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

All couplings meet exactly at $\Lambda_{\rm QCD}$ (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect)

Only $\alpha_3$ runs; EW couplings frozen (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: RGE: $\beta(g_i) = b_i g_i^3/(16\pi^2) + O(g^5)$ with $b_i$ depending on field content. SM at 1-loop: $b_1 = 41/10$ (U(1)$_Y$, GUT-normalised so $g_1 = \sqrt{5/3}g'$), $b_2 = -19/6$ (asymptotically free), $b_3 = -7$ (asymptotically free). Couplings at $m_Z$: $\alpha_1^{-1} \approx 59$, $\alpha_2^{-1} \approx 30$, $\alpha_3^{-1} \approx 8.5$. Run to high energy: three lines nearly meet, but SM precision is missed by few percent â€” visible 'fork' near $10^{15}$ GeV. MSSM: superpartners modify $b_i$ above SUSY scale, lines meet within precision at $M_{\rm GUT} \approx 2\times 10^{16}$ GeV. Major motivation for SUSY-GUT scenarios (SU(5), SO(10), E$_6$). Predictions: proton decay $p \to e^+\pi^0$ via X-boson exchange, $\tau_p \sim 10^{34-35}$ yr â€” searched at Super-K, future Hyper-K.

LFU violated in QED â€” well established (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
